meta.title: AI Engineer Roadmap — Top 1%
meta.subtitle: 26 Weeks · Full Depth · MERN Refresh + TypeScript + DevOps + ML Core + LLM Systems + Domain Impact
meta.philosophy: No skipping. No shortcuts. Every concept earned. Every project production-grade. Every week builds on the last. This is the roadmap that makes your knowledge unteatable.

background.gap: 1 year since active MERN work — full refresh needed on JS, Express, React, Mongoose, JWT, auth patterns

meta.gpuBudget: ₹4,000–7,000 total — Colab T4 + Kaggle free first, RunPod A100 only for DPO/vLLM weeks
meta.dsaNote: DSA removed per preference — focus is entirely on AI engineering depth

tracks.label: MERN + TypeScript + DevOps
tracks.desc: Full refresh from JS fundamentals through Express, React, MongoDB, PostgreSQL, then advance into TypeScript strict mode, Docker, Redis, BullMQ, WebSockets, Kafka, CI/CD, Prometheus, Grafana.

tracks.label: ML Fundamentals
tracks.desc: NumPy, PyTorch, transformers, embeddings, backprop from scratch, LoRA fine-tuning, DPO, quantization, vLLM. Understand what is inside every model you deploy.

tracks.label: LLM Engineering
tracks.desc: RAG pipelines, vector DBs, prompt engineering, evals, agents with LangGraph, multi-agent systems, vLLM inference ops, guardrails, observability.

weeks.theme: MERN Refresh Part 1 — JavaScript Deep Dive + Node.js + Express + MongoDB
weeks.note: 1 year gap means muscle memory is gone. Rebuild from fundamentals — not a skim, a proper refresh. Every concept you will use in AI engineering backends lives here.

mern: JavaScript core refresh: var/let/const scoping, hoisting, temporal dead zone
Functions: declaration vs expression vs arrow, this binding, call/apply/bind
Closures: what they are, why they matter, 3 practical examples
Prototypes: prototype chain, Object.create, class syntax as syntactic sugar
Practice: implement a debounce and throttle from scratch — both appear in real interviews
ai: Environment setup: Python 3.11, pip, venv, Jupyter, VS Code with Pylance
NumPy intro: ndarray creation, dtype, shape, reshape, indexing, slicing
Arithmetic operations: element-wise, broadcasting rules — understand the shapes
Build: implement matrix multiply using only NumPy dot product — verify against np.matmul

bonusRead.title: You Don't Know JS — Scope and Closures (Kyle Simpson)
bonusRead.why: The single best free resource for truly understanding JavaScript scoping. After a year away, closures and hoisting are the first things to get rusty. Read chapters 1-4 this week — it takes 2 hours and pays dividends forever.

mern: Async JavaScript: callbacks, Promises, async/await — understand the event loop
Promise.all, Promise.race, Promise.allSettled — when each is the right tool
Error handling: try/catch in async functions, unhandled rejection patterns
Event loop: call stack, task queue, microtask queue — draw it out
Practice: build a Promise.all from scratch — this is asked in interviews
ai: NumPy advanced: broadcasting deep dive — 5 examples with shape diagrams
einsum: understand the notation, implement matmul + batch matmul with it
Pandas: Series, DataFrame, read_csv, head, describe, info, dtypes
Pandas: loc vs iloc, boolean indexing, dropna, fillna, astype
Practice: load a CSV, compute group statistics, find missing values, export cleaned

bonusRead.title: Jake Archibald: In The Loop — Event Loop Visualised
bonusRead.why: The event loop is the most misunderstood concept in JavaScript and it appears in every senior JS interview. Jake's talk has the best visual explanation in existence. Watch it once, you will never be confused again.

mern: Node.js fundamentals: modules (CommonJS vs ESM), require vs import
Node.js built-ins: fs, path, os, events, stream — what each does
EventEmitter: implement a custom event system from scratch
Express setup: app.use, routing, middleware chain, error middleware (4 args)
Build: Express server with 5 routes, request logger middleware, 404 handler
ai: Pandas advanced: groupby, agg, pivot_table, merge (inner/outer/left/right)
Matplotlib: line chart, bar chart, scatter plot, histogram — 4 charts from one dataset
Seaborn: heatmap correlation matrix, pairplot — understand what they reveal
Practice: take a transactions CSV, answer 5 business questions with pandas + matplotlib

bonusRead.title: Node.js Event Loop — Official Docs Deep Dive
bonusRead.why: Node.js runs your Express backends, BullMQ workers, WebSocket servers, and Kafka consumers. Understanding phases — timers, I/O callbacks, poll, check — means you can reason about why your async code behaves the way it does.

mern: MongoDB refresh: documents, collections, BSON types, ObjectId
Mongoose: Schema, Model, types, validation, required, default, enum
CRUD: create, findOne, find with query, updateOne, deleteOne
Mongoose: virtuals, methods, statics, pre/post hooks (middleware)
Build: User + Post models with full CRUD routes in Express — validate all inputs
ai: Scikit-learn: train_test_split, Linear Regression, Logistic Regression
Evaluation: accuracy_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report, ROC-AUC
Pipeline: StandardScaler + LogisticRegression in sklearn Pipeline
Plot: ROC curve, precision-recall curve — understand what each tells you
Practice: train a logistic regression on any binary classification dataset, evaluate fully

bonusRead.title: MongoDB Schema Design Anti-Patterns — Official Blog
bonusRead.why: Your AI projects store eval results, agent traces, experiment configs, and LLM call logs in MongoDB. Getting schema design right from the start prevents painful migrations later. The 6 anti-patterns in this post are things most engineers learn the hard way.

mern: JWT authentication: what a JWT is, header.payload.signature structure
bcrypt: why hash passwords, salt rounds, hash + compare — implement from scratch
Auth middleware: verify JWT on protected routes, attach user to req
Refresh tokens: why they exist, short-lived access + long-lived refresh pattern
Build: full auth system — register, login, protected route, refresh token endpoint
ai: What is a neural network? Neurons, activation functions (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh), layers
Forward pass: input → hidden → output, matrix multiply at each layer
Loss functions: MSE for regression, cross-entropy for classification — derive both
Backpropagation intuition: chain rule, gradients, why we update weights
Implement: 2-layer MLP in raw NumPy — forward pass + manual gradient computation

bonusRead.title: The Illustrated Transformer — But First, Understand Backprop (3Blue1Brown)
bonusRead.why: 3Blue1Brown's backpropagation video is the clearest visual explanation of the most important algorithm in deep learning. Watch it before any PyTorch tutorial — understanding what .backward() is doing changes everything about how you debug training.

mern: Rebuild your payment wallet backend from scratch — no copy-pasting old code
Stack: Express + MongoDB/Mongoose + JWT auth + bcrypt
Features: register, login, create account, deposit, withdraw, transfer between users
ACID: use MongoDB sessions for transfer — atomic debit + credit, rollback on failure
Validation: Joi or express-validator on every input — no unvalidated data
Error handling: centralised error middleware, structured error responses
Push to GitHub with a clear README explaining the ACID transaction approach
ai: PyTorch intro: tensors vs NumPy arrays, .to(device), requires_grad
Autograd: what a computation graph is, how .backward() traverses it
Implement the same 2-layer MLP in PyTorch — compare with your NumPy version
Training loop: optimizer.zero_grad(), loss.backward(), optimizer.step()
Train on MNIST digit classification — get to > 95% accuracy
Plot loss curve and accuracy curve — does it look healthy? Debug if not
Journal: what did rebuilding the payment wallet teach you that you had forgotten?

bonusReads.title: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks — Andrej Karpathy
bonusReads.why: The best guide to debugging neural networks that refuse to train. You will hit a network that does not converge this week. Read this before spending 3 hours on a bug that is actually a learning rate issue.

bonusReads.title: MongoDB Transactions — Official ACID Guide
bonusReads.why: Your payment wallet uses MongoDB sessions for ACID transactions. Understanding exactly what MongoDB guarantees — and does not guarantee — means your transfer logic is actually correct, not just apparently correct.

bonusReads.title: Express.js Best Practices — Production Checklist
bonusReads.why: Every AI product you build will have an Express backend. Production best practices — compression, error handling, security headers, process management — should be habits from day one, not retrofitted later.

mern: REST API design: resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, versioning (/api/v1/)
Pagination: cursor-based vs offset — when each is correct (cursor for large datasets)
API documentation: Swagger/OpenAPI spec — document your payment wallet API
Postman: create a full collection for your wallet API — test every endpoint
Deploy to Render: set environment variables properly, test live endpoints
ai: CNNs: filters as feature detectors, pooling, feature maps — build intuition
Conv2d in PyTorch: kernel_size, stride, padding — understand each parameter
Build a small CNN on CIFAR-10 — aim for > 70% accuracy on free Colab T4
Understand: why does a CNN beat an MLP on images? What does each filter learn?
Visualise: plot 16 learned filters from first conv layer — what do they detect?

bonusReads.title: REST API Design Best Practices — Microsoft Azure Docs
bonusReads.why: Every AI product has a REST API. Microsoft's guide covers resource naming, versioning, error responses, and pagination — the decisions you make once and live with forever.

bonusReads.title: CS231n: Convolutional Neural Networks — Stanford Notes
bonusReads.why: Stanford's CS231n notes on CNNs are the gold standard reference. The visual explanations of convolution, pooling, and receptive fields are clearer than any tutorial. CNNs underlie vision models you will use in Phase 5.

weeks.theme: MERN Refresh Part 2 — React + State Management + PostgreSQL + Full-Stack Integration
weeks.note: React patterns, hooks, state management, and PostgreSQL — then wire everything together into a full-stack app you can actually show. This week ends with a deployed, working product.

mern: React refresh: JSX, components, props, children, key prop and why it matters
useState: what state is, why mutation does not trigger re-render, functional updates
useEffect: dependency array, cleanup function, common pitfalls (infinite loops)
useRef: mutable refs vs DOM refs — when each is appropriate
Build: a multi-step form with validation using only useState and useEffect
ai: HuggingFace Transformers: install, from transformers import pipeline
Run: sentiment analysis, NER, zero-shot classification, question answering
Understand: what tokenization does — encode a sentence, see the token IDs
AutoTokenizer: encode, decode, attention_mask, padding, truncation
Build: compare tokenization of the same sentence across 3 different models

bonusRead.title: A Complete Guide to useEffect — Dan Abramov
bonusRead.why: Dan Abramov is the co-creator of React. This post is the definitive guide to useEffect — stale closures, dependency arrays, cleanup. After a year away this is the one thing most likely to cause mysterious bugs.

mern: Advanced hooks: useCallback, useMemo — what they actually do and when they help
Custom hooks: extract logic into reusable hooks — build useFetch, useLocalStorage
useContext: prop drilling problem, createContext, useContext, Provider pattern
React Router v6: Routes, Route, useNavigate, useParams, useSearchParams, Outlet
Build: multi-page app — dashboard, transactions list, transaction detail with React Router
ai: Transformers deep dive: model.forward(), logits, softmax to probabilities
Tokenizer internals: BPE tokenization, vocabulary, special tokens ([CLS], [SEP], [PAD])
BERT architecture: encoder-only, bidirectional attention, [CLS] for classification
GPT architecture: decoder-only, causal/unidirectional attention, autoregressive generation
Build: run BERT and GPT-2 on the same sentence — compare outputs, understand why they differ

bonusRead.title: The Illustrated BERT — Jay Alammar
bonusRead.why: BERT is the foundation of every domain-specific NLP model in Phase 5 — BioBERT, LEGAL-BERT, DistilBERT. Understanding the architecture once, deeply, means every fine-tuning project is straightforward.

mern: State management: when local state is enough vs when you need something global
Zustand: create store, selectors, actions — simpler than Redux for AI projects
React Query (TanStack Query): useQuery, useMutation, cache, stale time, refetch
React Query is the right tool for server state — understand the distinction
Build: add Zustand for auth state + React Query for all API calls in your wallet frontend
ai: sentence-transformers: model.encode(), cosine_similarity, semantic search
Embeddings intuition: similar sentences → similar vectors in high-dimensional space
Build: find most similar FAQ answer to any user question — 30 lines of Python
Visualise: t-SNE or UMAP to project 100 sentence embeddings into 2D — see clusters
Understand: why cosine similarity, not Euclidean distance, for embeddings

bonusRead.title: Zustand vs Redux — When State Management Gets Complex
bonusRead.why: AI dashboards have complex UI state — experiment configs, real-time metrics, agent traces, running jobs. Zustand is the right tool for this complexity without Redux boilerplate. Understanding when to reach for it saves architectural mistakes.

mern: PostgreSQL refresh: tables, schemas, data types, primary keys, foreign keys
SQL: SELECT with WHERE, JOIN (inner/left/right), GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT
Indexes: btree, when to add one, EXPLAIN ANALYZE — understand query plans
Transactions: BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK, isolation levels (read committed, repeatable read)
pg library in Node.js: Pool, parameterised queries — never string concatenate SQL
ai: ChromaDB: create collection, add documents with metadata, query by similarity
Pinecone: create index, upsert vectors, query with top_k and metadata filters
Build: same 100-sentence corpus in both — compare query results and latency
Understand: what HNSW index is — approximate nearest neighbour search intuition
Understand: when to use Pinecone (production scale) vs ChromaDB (local dev)

bonusRead.title: Use The Index, Luke — SQL Performance Explained
bonusRead.why: Your AI projects store millions of eval results, LLM call logs, and experiment metrics in PostgreSQL. Understanding how indexes work — B-tree structure, selectivity, covering indexes — means your queries stay fast as data grows.

mern: Axios: interceptors for auth headers and error handling, retry logic
Environment variables: .env, dotenv, never commit secrets — .gitignore patterns
CORS: what it is, why it exists, how to configure it correctly in Express
File uploads: multer middleware — handle PDF and image uploads for AI projects
Build: add file upload to wallet — upload a receipt image, store metadata in MongoDB
ai: OpenAI API: chat completions — roles (system, user, assistant), temperature, max_tokens
OpenAI embeddings: text-embedding-ada-002 — cost, dimensions, rate limits
Build first real RAG: embed 20 wallet FAQ answers → store in ChromaDB → answer user questions
Understand: why does RAG reduce hallucination? What does grounding mean technically?
Log every API call: tokens used, latency, cost — build the cost tracking habit early

bonusRead.title: Building RAG Applications — A Complete Guide
bonusRead.why: Your first RAG system ships today. This guide covers the production concerns — chunking, retrieval quality, latency, cost, evaluation — that tutorials skip. Read it as you build.

mern: Build the full-stack payment wallet — frontend + backend integrated
React frontend: login, register, dashboard (balance, recent transactions), transfer form
Real-time: WebSocket notification when a transfer is received (Socket.io)
PostgreSQL: migrate your backend to use PostgreSQL instead of MongoDB
Both stores: use MongoDB for user sessions/logs, PostgreSQL for financial transactions — understand why
Deploy: backend to Render, frontend to Vercel — fully live
Write README with architecture diagram explaining why you chose each database
ai: RAG deep dive: chunking strategies — fixed-size (256, 512, 1024 tokens), sentence splitting
Chunk overlap: why it matters — experiment with 0%, 10%, 20% overlap
Retrieval metrics: precision@k, recall@k — evaluate your wallet FAQ RAG
Add RAG to wallet: 'what are all my transactions above ₹500 this month?' in natural language
LangSmith: sign up free, add tracing to every LangChain call — see every prompt and token
Journal: where did retrieval fail? Retrieval failure vs generation failure — how do you tell?

bonusReads.title: Socket.io — Real-Time Bidirectional Communication
bonusReads.why: Real-time features appear in every AI product — streaming LLM tokens, live agent logs, eval progress, fraud alerts. Socket.io's rooms, namespaces, and reconnection logic are patterns you will use repeatedly.

bonusReads.title: Chunking Strategies for LLM Applications — Pinecone
bonusReads.why: Chunking is the most underestimated decision in RAG. Wrong chunk size causes retrieval failures that look like LLM failures. Most candidates cannot explain why they chose their chunk size — you will be able to.

bonusReads.title: PostgreSQL vs MongoDB — The Real Comparison for Full-Stack Developers
bonusReads.why: You are using both in the same project. Understanding the genuine tradeoffs — ACID guarantees, schema flexibility, query patterns, JOIN performance — means you can justify your architecture choices in any interview.

mern: Testing fundamentals: unit tests vs integration tests vs e2e tests — when each
Jest: describe, it, expect, beforeEach, afterEach — set up for your backend
Supertest: HTTP integration tests — test every route in your wallet API
Write 10 tests: register, login, deposit, transfer (success), transfer (insufficient funds)
Code coverage: jest --coverage — aim for > 80% on critical paths
ai: Transformers attention mechanism: Q, K, V — what they represent geometrically
Multi-head attention: why multiple heads? What does each head potentially learn?
Positional encoding: sinusoidal — why position information must be injected explicitly
Watch: Andrej Karpathy 'Attention Is All You Need explained' section of his GPT video
Build: implement single-head self-attention in NumPy — Q, K, V matrices, softmax, output

bonusReads.title: The Illustrated Transformer — Jay Alammar
bonusReads.why: Every AI engineer has read this. If you have not, you will feel left out of conversations at every interview. Read it today — it takes 45 minutes and explains the architecture that underlies every LLM you will ever work with.

bonusReads.title: Jest + Supertest Testing Guide — LogRocket
bonusReads.why: Untested AI backends fail in production in ways that are very hard to debug. Testing habits formed now compound — every project you add to your portfolio will have tests, signalling professional-grade engineering.

bonusReads.title: Attention Is All You Need — Original Paper
bonusReads.why: Read only the abstract, introduction, and Figure 1. Understanding the original architecture from the source — not a tutorial — gives you the credibility and vocabulary that sets you apart in deep technical interviews.

weeks.theme: TypeScript + Docker + Redis + BullMQ + CI/CD + NumPy Deep Dive
weeks.note: TypeScript is non-negotiable in production AI codebases. Docker is how every AI service ships. Redis and BullMQ handle the async jobs every AI product needs. Build these as habits now.

mern: TypeScript fundamentals: static typing rationale, tsc compiler, tsconfig.json
Types vs interfaces: when each is appropriate, extending, merging
Generics: what they solve, simple generic functions and types
Utility types: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly — memorise these
Convert your wallet Express routes to TypeScript — fix every type error
ai: NumPy deep dive: strides, memory layout, views vs copies — why this matters for performance
Vectorisation: replace every Python for-loop with NumPy operations
einsum mastery: understand the notation fully — implement attention with einsum
Benchmark: loop vs vectorised vs einsum on 10M element operations — measure the speedup
Practice: implement softmax, cross-entropy loss, and sigmoid in pure NumPy

bonusRead.title: TypeScript Deep Dive — Basarat Ali
bonusRead.why: The best free TypeScript book available. Chapters on types vs interfaces, generics, and strict mode are essential reading before you start using TypeScript in production. AI pipelines pass complex nested objects — untyped code creates silent bugs.

mern: TypeScript strict mode: strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes
Type narrowing: typeof, instanceof, in operator, discriminated unions
Enums vs const objects — why const objects are often better
Async TypeScript: Promise<T>, async functions with proper return types
Convert your Mongoose models to TypeScript with proper interfaces
ai: PyTorch deep dive: Dataset, DataLoader, custom collate_fn, pin_memory
Training loop best practices: gradient clipping, weight decay, learning rate scheduling
CosineAnnealingLR, StepLR, ReduceLROnPlateau — when to use each
Model checkpointing: save/load state_dict — understand what is in a checkpoint
Build: complete training pipeline with validation, early stopping, and model saving

bonusRead.title: TypeScript Strict Mode — Why Every Bug It Catches Is Real
bonusRead.why: Strict mode is not pedantic — it catches real production bugs. In AI pipelines, a null pointer from an untyped API response can silently corrupt an entire eval run. Strict mode makes this a compile-time error instead of a 3am production issue.

mern: Docker fundamentals: images vs containers, Dockerfile instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD, ENTRYPOINT)
Layer caching: why order matters — COPY package.json before COPY . is not optional
Multi-stage builds: build stage + production stage — smaller final images
.dockerignore: what to exclude and why (node_modules, .env, .git)
Build: Dockerise your wallet backend — compare image sizes with/without multi-stage
ai: PyTorch: GPU memory management, .to(device), cuda.empty_cache(), memory_allocated()
Batch size effect on training: large vs small batch — gradient noise, generalisation
Mixed precision training: torch.cuda.amp.autocast() — faster training, less memory
Gradient accumulation: simulate large batch on small GPU — implement it
Build: train your CIFAR-10 CNN with mixed precision — measure speedup vs baseline

bonusRead.title: Docker Layer Caching — Official Best Practices
bonusRead.why: Every AI project eventually Dockerizes a service that installs heavy dependencies (torch, transformers, llama-cpp). Wrong layer order means 15-minute rebuilds on every code change. Correct caching means 30-second rebuilds. This matters every day.

mern: Docker Compose: multi-container apps, services, networks, volumes, depends_on, healthcheck
Compose your wallet: backend + MongoDB + PostgreSQL + Redis — one command startup
Redis fundamentals: strings, hashes, lists, sorted sets, TTL, EXPIRE
Redis patterns: caching (cache-aside), session storage, pub/sub
Rate limiter: implement token bucket rate limiting in Redis — your gateway project needs this
ai: HuggingFace PEFT: LoRA intuition — low-rank matrices, rank parameter, trainable vs frozen
LoraConfig: r, lora_alpha, target_modules, lora_dropout — understand each parameter
get_peft_model, print_trainable_parameters — see how few params LoRA trains
Load TinyLlama or Phi-3-mini on Colab T4 — compare base vs LoRA trainable params
Understand: why LoRA works — the hypothesis that weight updates live in low-rank space

bonusRead.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models — Original Paper
bonusRead.why: Read sections 1, 2, and 4. LoRA is the foundation of all efficient fine-tuning. Every interview at a company doing fine-tuning will ask you to explain it. The paper is short and readable — understanding it from the source takes 45 minutes.

mern: BullMQ: queues, workers, jobs, priorities, retries, backoff, dead letter queue
Add to wallet: async email notifications via BullMQ — job queued on every transaction
GitHub Actions: CI pipeline — install, lint, type-check, test on every push to main
Auto-deploy: trigger Render deployment on successful merge to main
Jest coverage gate: fail CI if coverage drops below 80% on critical paths
ai: Quantization: what it means to go from FP32 → FP16 → INT8 → INT4
BitsAndBytes: load_in_8bit=True, load_in_4bit=True — run a quantized model on Colab
GGUF format: what llama.cpp uses — why quantization enables local inference
Ollama: install, run llama3 or mistral locally — no GPU needed
Compare: GPT-4 vs local quantized mistral on 5 prompts — quality vs cost tradeoff

bonusRead.title: A Gentle Introduction to 8-bit Matrix Multiplication — HuggingFace
bonusRead.why: Quantization is how you run large models on affordable hardware — critical for Phase 5 projects. Understanding the quality-cost tradeoff is a production decision you will make repeatedly. This post explains it without heavy math.

mern: Upgrade wallet to TypeScript — full strict mode, no any types allowed
Add Docker Compose: wallet backend + MongoDB + PostgreSQL + Redis
Add BullMQ: async email worker for transaction notifications
GitHub Actions CI: lint + typecheck + test + coverage gate
Prometheus: instrument wallet API — request count, latency histogram per route
Grafana: connect to Prometheus, build 4-panel dashboard (requests/sec, p95 latency, error rate, active users)
Deploy updated stack to Render — verify CI/CD pipeline end to end
ai: RAG pipeline deep dive: LangChain LCEL chains, prompt templates, output parsers
LangChain LCEL: compose chains with | operator — understand the runnable interface
Build: PDF → chunk → embed → store → retrieve → answer with source citations
LangSmith: trace every step — see exactly what prompt was sent, what tokens used
Ragas intro: install, run faithfulness + answer_relevancy on 5 Q&A pairs
Journal: where does your RAG fail? Write down 3 specific failure cases you observed

bonusReads.title: Prometheus Best Practices — Rob Ewaschuk
bonusReads.why: You will instrument multiple AI services with Prometheus over the next 20 weeks. Understanding counters vs gauges vs histograms means your metrics are actually useful — not just noise on a dashboard.

bonusReads.title: LangChain LCEL Deep Dive — Official Guide
bonusReads.why: LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) is the modern way to build LangChain applications. The | pipe operator, Runnable interface, and streaming support are used in every serious LangChain codebase. Understanding it before building saves confusion.

bonusReads.title: Ragas: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
bonusReads.why: You will build an eval engine on top of Ragas. Reading the paper means you understand what faithfulness, answer relevancy, context recall, and context precision actually measure — not just how to call the library.

mern: NestJS: modules, controllers, providers, dependency injection — why it exists vs Express
Build a simple NestJS CRUD API — understand the module system
Compare: NestJS vs Express — when each is the right choice for AI backends
System Design intro: how to approach a system design question (requirements → estimation → design → deep dive)
Design your wallet at 100k users — what breaks first? How do you fix it?
ai: LangGraph intro: state machines, nodes, edges, conditional routing, checkpointing
Build: simple 3-node LangGraph (Planner → Executor → Reviewer) — trace in LangSmith
Understand: why LangGraph over basic LangChain agents — state persistence, cycles, branching
LangGraph state: TypedDict schema, how state flows through nodes, how to update it
Read: Anthropic's 'Building Effective Agents' guide — this is your agent architecture bible

bonusReads.title: LangGraph — Multi-Agent Workflows Official Guide
bonusReads.why: LangGraph is the backbone of Projects 3, 5, 9, and 11. Understanding the state machine model — nodes, edges, conditional routing, human-in-the-loop — before you build saves days of confusion.

bonusReads.title: Building Effective Agents — Anthropic Research
bonusReads.why: Anthropic's guide to building effective agents is the most practical resource from people who build frontier models. Read this cover to cover. It covers exactly the failure modes you will hit in Projects 3, 5, and 11.

weeks.theme: Advanced RAG + Vector DBs + LangChain + Kafka + WebSockets + Transformer Internals
weeks.note: This week closes the last remaining gaps before projects begin. Kafka, advanced RAG techniques, and transformer internals from first principles. Everything in Phases 2-5 builds directly on this week.

mern: WebSockets with Socket.io: connection lifecycle, rooms, namespaces, emit patterns
Server-Sent Events (SSE): when to use SSE over WebSockets — streaming LLM output
Implement SSE in Express: res.write with event format, connection management
React: consume SSE stream — show token-by-token LLM output in real time
Build: streaming LLM response viewer — user asks question, tokens stream to UI
ai: Embeddings deep dive: semantic space, cosine similarity, dot product, L2 distance
Why cosine similarity and not Euclidean for text: unit sphere, magnitude normalisation
Embedding model comparison: OpenAI ada-002 vs BAAI/bge-large vs sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM
MTEB leaderboard: understand the benchmark tasks — retrieval, classification, clustering, reranking
Build: same corpus, 3 different embedding models — which retrieves best? Measure precision@5

bonusRead.title: MTEB Leaderboard — Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
bonusRead.why: When an interviewer asks 'which embedding model did you use and why?' — this is where you go to justify your answer. Knowing the benchmark leaderboard signals serious engineering thinking, not cargo-culting the default model.

mern: Kafka fundamentals: topics, partitions, offsets, producers, consumers, consumer groups
Why Kafka and not just a database queue: log compaction, replay, ordering guarantees
Docker Compose: add Kafka + Zookeeper to your stack
kafka-node or kafkajs: produce and consume messages in Node.js
Build: wallet transaction producer — every transaction emits a Kafka event
ai: Advanced RAG: hybrid search — BM25 (keyword) + dense (semantic) combined
Reciprocal Rank Fusion: how to merge rankings from two different retrieval systems
Reranking: cross-encoder vs bi-encoder — why reranking improves over pure retrieval
Cohere Rerank API or sentence-transformers cross-encoder: add reranking to your RAG
Measure: retrieval precision before and after reranking — what improvement do you see?

bonusRead.title: Reranking — Why Two-Stage Retrieval Beats One-Stage
bonusRead.why: Reranking is the single highest-impact RAG improvement after basic retrieval works. Cheap embedding retrieval + expensive cross-encoder reranker is the production pattern used by every serious retrieval system.

mern: Kafka consumer: transaction events → feature computation → store aggregates in Redis
Dead letter queue: failed messages → separate topic → retry with exponential backoff
Zod: schema validation on all API inputs — never trust external data
Prisma ORM: compare with Mongoose — set up Prisma for PostgreSQL in wallet
Database migrations: up/down migrations with Prisma — version your schema changes
ai: Advanced chunking: parent-child chunking — index small chunks, return large parent context
Self-querying retrieval: LLM generates metadata filters from natural language query
HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings — embed a fake answer, retrieve on that
Build: test HyDE vs standard retrieval on your corpus — does it improve recall?
LangSmith: trace all retrieval experiments — compare latency and quality

bonusRead.title: HyDE: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval Without Relevance Labels
bonusRead.why: HyDE is a clever trick that often improves retrieval on complex questions. Read the paper abstract and Figure 1. When you implement it and it improves your Ragas scores, you will remember why the idea works.

mern: PM2: cluster mode, log rotation, memory monitoring, restart policies

bonusReads.title: How ChatGPT Was Actually Trained: The RLHF Story
bonusReads.preview: GPT-3 could generate text — but also said terrible things. The three-stage training process that turned a raw language model into a product used by 100 million people.

sections.heading: THE GPT-3 PROBLEM
sections.text: GPT-3 was trained to predict the next token on internet text — which means it also learned from toxic content. Ask it a question and it might continue the question rather than answering.

sections.heading: THREE STAGES OF RLHF
steps: SFT: Human labellers wrote ideal responses. GPT-3 was fine-tuned on these examples.
Reward Model: Labellers ranked multiple responses. A separate model learned to predict these rankings.
PPO: The SFT model is trained using the reward model as a signal. Higher-scoring responses get reinforced.

sections.heading: THE ALIGNMENT TAX
sections.callout: RLHF makes models more helpful but often reduces raw benchmark performance. OpenAI called this the alignment tax.
sections.text: InstructGPT scored lower on some benchmarks than GPT-3 but humans vastly preferred it. Benchmark performance and product usefulness are fundamentally different things.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: DPO achieves similar alignment without the RL training loop — just a clever loss function on preference pairs. When asked how you align a model — this is the context that makes your DPO answer credible.

bonusReads.title: Constitutional AI: How Anthropic Built Claude Without Human Feedback on Harmful Content
bonusReads.preview: RLHF requires humans to rate harmful outputs. Constitutional AI makes the model critique its own harmful outputs using a written constitution — no labellers needed.

sections.heading: THE PROBLEM
sections.text: To train a safe model via RLHF, you need humans to evaluate harmful content at scale — expensive and harmful to the workers doing the rating.

sections.heading: THE APPROACH
steps: Write a Constitution: principles the AI should follow.
SL-CAI: The model critiques and revises its own harmful outputs. Fine-tune on the revised outputs.
RLAIF: An AI model (not humans) scores outputs using the constitution as a rubric.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: Constitutional AI is why Claude exists. RLAIF is now standard thinking at Anthropic and Google DeepMind. When asked how to align an LLM without human RLHF labellers — this is the answer.

bonusReads.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning: Meta's Analysis of When Each Wins
bonusReads.preview: Meta ran a systematic comparison of RAG vs fine-tuning vs both combined. The results clarified when each approach actually works in practice.

sections.heading: THE RESULTS
steps: Knowledge updates frequently: RAG wins. Fine-tuning freezes knowledge at training time.
Tone and format changes: fine-tuning wins. RAG cannot reliably change output style or behaviour.
Deep domain reasoning: fine-tuning + RAG wins — both together.
Cost: RAG is approximately 10x cheaper than fine-tuning at scale.

sections.heading: THE DECISION FRAMEWORK
sections.callout: Use RAG when the knowledge changes. Use fine-tuning when the behaviour changes. Use both when you need deep domain reasoning over live data.
sections.text: This is the single most important AI product decision you will make repeatedly. Every senior AI engineering interview will ask it in some form.

bonusReads.title: How Stripe Uses Machine Learning to Fight Fraud: Radar Under the Hood
bonusReads.preview: Stripe processes hundreds of billions of dollars annually. Radar uses ML on transaction graphs, velocity features, and network signals — exactly what you built in Project 10.

sections.heading: THE CORE SIGNALS
steps: Velocity features: how many transactions from this card in the last hour?
Network signals: is this email connected to known fraudulent accounts through shared devices?
Behavioural signals: does this pattern match the cardholder's historical behaviour?

sections.heading: THE REAL-TIME CONSTRAINT
sections.callout: Stripe's fraud decision must complete in < 100ms — fast enough to not add perceptible latency to checkout flows.
sections.text: This drives every architecture decision: Redis feature caches, pre-computed embeddings, decision cascades from cheap rules to expensive models.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: When applying to Razorpay, PayU, or Juspay — referencing Stripe Radar's architecture and comparing it to your Project 10 implementation signals you understand production constraints, not just Kaggle benchmarks.

bonusReads.title: How GitHub Copilot Works: From Keystroke to Code Suggestion in 80ms
bonusReads.preview: Copilot processes your entire file, open tabs, and cursor position to generate suggestions in under 80ms. The infrastructure is a lesson in latency-constrained AI engineering.

sections.heading: THE CONTEXT WINDOW PROBLEM
steps: Current file: included verbatim.
Open tabs: ranked by recency and language similarity — top N included.
Related files: retrieved via embedding similarity to current context.
This is RAG applied to code — your Project 5 implements the same pattern.

sections.heading: THE 80ms CONSTRAINT
sections.callout: A suggestion arriving 200ms after a keystroke feels like lag. 80ms feels instant. Every engineering decision is optimised for this one number.
sections.text: This is why Copilot uses a smaller Codex model rather than GPT-4 — smaller model, faster inference, acceptable quality. The product constraint shapes the ML architecture choice.

sections.heading: FOR YOUR INTERVIEWS
sections.text: Your Project 5 is a less latency-constrained version of Copilot. Being able to explain both the similarities and differences — and why your architecture chose correctness over latency — shows product-level AI engineering thinking.

bonusReads.title: How Netflix Decides What to Show You: Two-Stage Retrieval and A/B Testing at Scale
bonusReads.preview: Netflix attributes 80% of content watched to their recommendation system. Understanding how they do it reveals the production patterns behind every embedding-based retrieval system.

sections.heading: TWO-STAGE RETRIEVAL
steps: Stage 1 (Retrieval): fast ANN search over 10,000+ titles using user embedding vs title embeddings — returns top 200 candidates.
Stage 2 (Ranking): expensive neural ranker scores 200 candidates using 100+ features — returns top 20 for display.
This is the same retrieve-then-rerank pattern in your RAG eval engine.

sections.heading: A/B TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE
sections.callout: Netflix runs 250+ A/B tests simultaneously. Every algorithm change is validated with online metrics before full rollout.
sections.text: Your Project 4 DPO A/B testing implements the same principle. Offline eval scores and live win rates are both needed — offline metrics alone can mislead.

bonusReads.title: How Uber's Two-Tower Neural Net Matches Riders to Drivers in 2 Seconds
bonusReads.preview: Every Uber match is a real-time ML ranking problem using the same architecture that powers RAG retrieval.

sections.heading: THE ARCHITECTURE
steps: Rider request fires — Feature Store returns pre-computed features instantly.
Two-tower net: one tower encodes the rider, one encodes each candidate driver as embeddings.
Dot product similarity ranks candidates — top-K go to a reranker.
Reranker applies business constraints: driver earnings fairness, surge price cap.

sections.heading: WHY THIS MATTERS FOR AI ENGINEERS
sections.text: Two-tower models are the industry standard for large-scale retrieval. One tower = query, one tower = documents. This is exactly how your RAG vector search works. Same architecture, completely different domain.

bonusReads.title: How Notion AI Ships: Streaming, Context Management, and Knowing When Not to Use AI
bonusReads.preview: The most important metric for AI writing is time-to-first-token. Notion obsessed over showing the first word in under 300ms.

sections.heading: THE UX INSIGHT
sections.text: If the user sees the first word in 300ms, the AI feels fast. If they wait 2 seconds for the first word, it feels broken — even if total response time is the same. Perceived responsiveness is the metric.

sections.heading: WHEN NOT TO USE AI
sections.callout: The hardest product decision was choosing what NOT to make AI. Spell check, bullet formatting, basic grammar — deterministic rules are cheaper, faster, and more reliable.
sections.text: Knowing when NOT to invoke an LLM is a core AI engineering judgment. A regex is cheaper and more reliable than a 200ms LLM call for structured operations.
